children.text: Eightieth session – Agenda item 177 – Financing of the United Nations Support Office in Haiti – Budgets for the periods from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 and from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 for the United Nations Support Office in Haiti – Report of the Secretary-General
Contents

table_content.text: Planning assumptions and mission support initiatives
Planning assumptions and mission support initiatives
Planning assumptions and mission support initiatives
Analysis of requirements for air operations
Analysis of requirements for air operations
Analysis of requirements for air operations
Actions to be taken by the General Assembly
Actions to be taken by the General Assembly
Actions to be taken by the General Assembly
The present report contains the budgets for the United Nations Support Office in Haiti (UNSOH) for the periods from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 and from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027.

The Security Council, in its resolution 2793 (2025), requested the Secretary-General to establish UNSOH, to provide logistical support to the Gang Suppression Force; mission support services to the United Nations Integrated Office in Haiti; in-theatre medical evacuation for the Haitian National Police and the Haitian Armed Forces on joint operations with the Gang Suppression Force; and technical support to the Organization of American States. During the 2025/26 and 2026/27 periods, UNSOH, headquartered in Port-au-Prince, will initiate a multi-year construction project to establish the infrastructure necessary to support the Force; implement risk prevention and mitigation measures, especially pertaining to wastewater management as it relates to waterborne disease risk to ensure that it can be safely discharged in accordance with United Nations standards; deploy air and ground transportation assets, as well as equipment, in particular information and communications technology equipment; and establish the necessary medical infrastructure and medical evacuation capacity.

Financial resources

	The budget proposed for the 2025/26 period amounts to $271,328,700. The budget proposed for 2026/27 period in the amount of $403,583,200 represents an increase of $132,254,500, or 48.7 per cent, compared with the proposed budget for the 2025/26 period.
(Thousands of United States dollars; budget year is from 1 July to 30 June)
The proposed budget provides for the deployment of uniformed personnel and civilian staff, as follows:

a	Represents the highest level of authorized/proposed strength.
	b	The authorized strength for uniformed personnel for the Gang Suppression Force is 5,500. For planning purposes, the table shows the distribution of military contingent personnel and formed police unit personnel.
	c	Funded under general temporary assistance.
	d	Includes international and national United Nations Volunteers.

Legislative history
(Thousands of United States dollars)

a		Includes the previously approved amounts of $54.2 million and $50.0 million in strategic deployment stocks. 
	b	The total amount has been assessed on Member States.
The actions to be taken by the General Assembly are set out in section IV of the present report.

children.text: A.	Mandate

1.	The mandate of UNSOH was established by the Security Council in its resolution 2793 (2025). In the resolution, the Council authorized Member States to transition the Multinational Security Support Mission, as authorized in resolution 2699 (2023) and renewed in resolution 2751 (2024), to the Gang Suppression Force, in close cooperation and coordination with the Government of Haiti, for an initial period of 12 months, comprising an authorized personnel ceiling of 5,550 for the Gang Suppression Force, consisting of 5,500 uniformed personnel, comprising both military and police personnel, and 50 civilians.

2.	The Security Council noted the assessment by the Secretary-General, contained in his letter to the President of the Security Council (S/2025/122), that a United Nations support office should provide comprehensive logistical and operational support to the Gang Suppression Force and, owing to the unique character of the Force, requested the Secretary-General to establish the United Nations Support Office in Haiti (UNSOH) to provide support, primarily to the Gang Suppression Force, the United Nations Integrated Office in Haiti (BINUH), the Haitian National Police and the Haitian Armed Forces on any joint operations with the Gang Suppression Force, as well as technical support to the Organization of American States (OAS), with a view to assuming full logistical support responsibility for the Gang Suppression Force within six months of the adoption of resolution 2793 (2025). UNSOH will provide, on a cost-recovery basis, the standard range of mission support services to BINUH in support of the delivery of its mandate, as extended in resolution 2814 (2026), with a view to assuming full responsibility by 1 February 2026.

3.	The Standing Group of Partners was established by seven Member States to provide, in consultation with the Government of Haiti, high-level strategic direction, oversight and relevant political decision-making for the Gang Suppression Force, as appropriate, while operational command and day-to-day operational decision-making will remain with the Force Commander, to be selected by the Standing Group. The Group is also responsible for leading on securing voluntary financial contributions for personnel reimbursements, facilitating force generation and providing strategic representation and coordination for the Force. A Special Representative for the Gang Suppression Force, based in Port-au-Prince, has been selected by the Standing Group to provide strategic representation and coordination.

4.	Through its resolution 2793 (2025), the Security Council established UNSOH as a separate entity in Haiti, with dedicated leadership. The Head of UNSOH, while retaining delegated authority for the planning and delivery of operational support, in line with paragraphs 10 and 11 of resolution 2793 (2025), will ensure strong coordination with the Special Representative of the Secretary-General for Haiti and Head of BINUH, who provides overall strategic guidance for the United Nations presence in the country. UNSOH will maintain close and sustained collaboration, coordination and information-sharing on support provided to the Gang Suppression Force among all United Nations political, development, humanitarian and financial entities, as well as among local and international partners operating in Haiti, as appropriate, in order to prevent duplication of efforts and to maximize the leveraging of existing resources. Support provided by UNSOH to the Gang Suppression Force, the Haitian National Police, the Haitian Armed Forces and OAS will be in full compliance with the human rights due diligence policy on United Nations support to non-United Nations security forces.
B.	Planning assumptions and mission support initiatives

5.	Since the assassination of the President of Haiti, Jovenel Moise, in July 2021, Haiti has undergone a fragile political transition, which has created space for gangs to challenge State authority. Gang-related violence killed at least 5,601 people in 2024, as gang attacks have become more organized, reflecting greater planning, tactical sophistication and access to heavy weaponry. Strategic infrastructure, such as electrical plants, factories, fibre-optic cables and fuel facilities, is a frequent target of attacks, as are hospitals and schools.

6.	The Port-au-Prince metropolitan area is largely isolated, as commercial flights remain suspended into and out of the country’s main international airport and as gangs control all main roads into and out of the capital. The escalating violence has severely hampered the international presence in Port-au-Prince.

7.	In 2025, gang expansion beyond metropolitan Port-au-Prince continued to undermine State authority and disrupted humanitarian and commercial routes. Armed violence intensified in both urban and peri-urban areas, with gangs deploying heavy-calibre weapons and conducting coordinated multi-route assaults.

8.	Gang violence remains widespread. Intentional homicides targeted individuals suspected of cooperating with the police or resisting gang control, while sexual violence, predominantly against women and girls was used as a punitive tactic, with reported cases increasing following police operations in November 2025. Gangs continued to engage in kidnapping for ransom, extortion and the destruction of property, obstructing access to essential services, including healthcare and education. Reports also indicated an increase in the trafficking in children, with children continuing to be used by gangs in multiple roles, including in violent attacks.

9.	The outlook for 2026 in Haiti reflects both opportunities for progress and persistent challenges. While efforts are under way to strengthen international security support, violence has intensified and expanded geographically, exacerbating food insecurity and instability, as transitional governance arrangements near expiry and overdue elections remain urgent.

10.	The requirements for the 2025/26 period, from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026, are based on deploying and providing support to 1,500 military contingent personnel and 140 formed police unit personnel, starting on 1 April 2026, and on deploying an additional 1,860 contingent personnel by 1 July 2026. The requirements for the 2026/27 period are based on providing support to 3,360 military contingent personnel and 140 formed police unit personnel for six months, from 1 July to 31 December 2026, and 4,940 military contingent personnel and 560 formed police personnel for six months, from 1 January to 30 June 2027.

11.	The key elements of the logistical support provided by UNSOH to the Gang Suppression Force include: (a) the provision of rations, fuel, water, accommodation (office and living accommodation), and all necessary infrastructure to include the primary base of the Gang Suppression Force and any forward bases, medical support, mobility support for personnel and equipment to include ground transportation, aviation and troop rotation, strategic communications, information technology, and geographic information system (GIS) support; (b) the reimbursement of contingent-owned equipment in line with the United Nations contingent-owned framework and rates and practices; and (c) supporting joint operations centres and GIS support to enable targeted and intelligence-led operations and, to the extent possible and without detracting from support to day-to-day operations, provide support to the Haitian National Police, the Haitian Armed Forces and the Gang Suppression Force, as appropriate, in taking measures to implement the objectives of the United Nations arms embargo.

12.	UNSOH will also provide: (a) the standard range of mission support services to BINUH, on a cost-recovery basis, in support of the delivery of its mandate; (b) in-theatre medical evacuation for the Haitian National Police and the Haitian Armed Forces on joint operations with the Gang Suppression Force, on an exceptional and cost-recovery basis; and (c) technical support to OAS for it to provide a targeted support package for the Haitian National Police on joint operations with the Gang Suppression Force, including the provision of food and water, fuel, transport, tents, defence stores and appropriate communications equipment to enable interoperability with the Force, to be funded by voluntary contributions, including from the OAS SECURE-Haiti project.

13.	To ensure that all UNSOH support is compliant with the human rights due diligence policy, an appropriately sized capacity will be developed in UNSOH. This capacity is consistent in approach, structure and function for functions related to the human rights due diligence policy in a support office, as also proposed for the United Nations Support Office in Somalia. This capacity will work in close coordination with the rule of law component at BINUH, as appropriate, and benefit from close cooperation with and support from the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights.

14.	The Head of UNSOH will have separate quantifiable compacts with both the Head of BINUH for the delivery of support to BINUH and the Special Representative for the Gang Suppression Force, in consultation with the Force Commander, for the delivery of support to the Force.

15.	The Support Office will be headquartered in Port-au-Prince, with support bases in the capital and in nearby locations within Haiti. An office has also been established in Santo Domingo for non-location-dependent functions, including human resources and financial transactional support.

16.	The requirements take into account the milestones outlined in resolution 2793 (2025), including the provision of support to Gang Suppression Force starting on 1 April 2026 and the anticipated deployment of the Force. They are based on high-level indicative estimates, taking into consideration peacekeeping and support office experience; average costs across medium-sized and large peacekeeping missions; previous peacekeeping missions in Haiti; and the United Nations Support Office in Somalia, adjusted to take into consideration the operating environment in Haiti.

17.	Security arrangements will be governed by the security risk management measures for Haiti and will balance assessed risks with continued programme delivery. The arrangements will be regularly revised and updated to reflect the changed operational environment during and after the establishment of UNSOH. The protection of United Nations personnel, premises and assets will remain a core priority. The Gang Suppression Force will contribute to the safety and security of all United Nations personnel, including through security escorts. In order to allow additional United Nations personnel to deploy to Haiti, a dedicated passenger aircraft will be positioned in Santo Domingo to carry out evacuations, should the security environment require it.

18.	The proposed staffing complement of 419 civilian personnel will allow for the recruitment of key capacities and critical start-up functions, with personnel deployed on a phased basis, taking into consideration security arrangements and priority functions. A 12-months adjusted vacancy rate of 75 per cent has been applied in the 2025/26 period, and a 30 per cent rate has been applied in the 2026/27 period, on the basis of anticipated recruitment timelines. The requirements for civilian staffing take into account the amount projected to be recovered from BINUH for the provision of mission support services. On the basis of an initial estimate, which will be refined in the budget for the 2026/27 period, this amount is expected to be about 15 per cent lower than the cost that would be incurred by BINUH to provide the same services in-house, resulting in an efficiency gain of $252,700 in the 2025/26 period and $707,600 in the 2026/27 period.

19.	A multi-year project is being initiated to establish infrastructure to support 5,500 uniformed personnel in locations determined under the concept of operations of the Gang Suppression Force. Construction, including security enhancements for compounds in Port-au-Prince, will start during the 2025/26 period and will extend to temporary and forward operating bases during the 2026/27 period. The project will include the establishment and upgrading of existing infrastructure to provide accommodation, office space, medical facilities and warehouses and the construction of camps and forward operating bases, as well as road improvements. It will cover all establishment activities, including ground clearing, perimeter security, hardwall structures and fit-out, and the installation of treatment plants. Single-year projects for the 2026/27 period include the development of movement control terminals at the seaport and bunkers and ammunition dump storage areas in Port-au-Prince and the regions.

20.	As part of the multi-year infrastructure project, permanent infrastructure to be used by the Gang Suppression Force will be established, with the design and construction to be carried out in consultation with the Haitian authorities. This infrastructure will be handed over to the Government, in accordance with United Nations policies, as a positive legacy, upon completion of the mandate of the Force.

21.	The Security Council also called upon Member States participating in the Gang Suppression Force to adopt appropriate water and wastewater management and other environmental controls to guard against the introduction and spread of waterborne diseases, in cooperation with the Haitian authorities, which bear shared responsibility for guarding against waterborne disease.

22.	Risk prevention and mitigation measures, especially pertaining to wastewater management as it relates to waterborne disease risk, have been integrated from the onset of the Support Office in Haiti. Context-adapted wastewater treatment plants and waste management yards will be implemented at all the main UNSOH bases and at the Gang Suppression Force headquarters. UNSOH has been included in the scope of the Department of Operational Support strategy, The Way Forward: Environment Strategy for Peace Operations 2023–2030, and fully integrated into the environmental risk and performance framework, including in the online Environmental Action Planning and Performance platform. In collaboration with UNSOH, the Department has developed initial energy, waste and wastewater management plans, which incorporate best practices and which will be updated annually to guide performance improvement over time.

23.	Within the country’s volatile environment, the operational footprint of the Gang Suppression Force and UNSOH support elements will be concentrated in Port-au-Prince and surrounding communes, where the likelihood of armed contact and mass-casualty incidents is greatest. The country’s medical infrastructure is limited to basic stabilization and primary care, with no sustainable level III capability. The Support Office will rely on a dedicated level II-plus facility, which was established during the 2025/26 period and will be expanded during the 2026/27 period in view of the increased footprint of the Force. The facility will be equipped for damage control surgery and intensive care, supported by a casualty staging unit and strategic medical evacuation to regional hubs, including Santo Domingo.

24.	A fixed-wing air ambulance with guaranteed availability was initially deployed to support the Multinational Security Support Mission. This aircraft will be positioned in Santo Domingo to support the Gang Suppression Force and UNSOH, with patients to be transported to a higher-level facility in the Dominican Republic.

25.	Aviation support will be provided through the deployment of two civilian medium utility rotary-wing aircraft, one in January and one in March 2026, and one fixed-wing passenger aircraft for security purposes, to be deployed during the 2026/27 period, supplemented by the use of on-call arrangements. UNSOH will also provide support for the deployment of eight military utility rotary-wing aircraft to the Gang Suppression Force by the end of damage-control surgery June 2026. UNSOH personnel will initially be deployed to Port-au-Prince by a commercially contracted civilian helicopter from Santo Domingo. Strategic aviation requirements, including aircraft contractual arrangements and troop rotations, will be managed in cooperation with United Nations Headquarters in New York and the United Nations Logistics Base at Brindisi, Italy.

26.	Three tranches of equipment from the strategic deployment stocks will arrive in Port-au-Prince by the end of March 2026, with additional shipments planned for the 2026/27 period, as the Support Office and its equipment reception capability expands. Efforts are under way, to the extent possible, to transfer assets from closing and downsizing missions. In line with the applicable regulations and rules, the assets that are economical to be transferred, namely, assets in good condition and whose value and remaining useful life is worth the cost of transfer and associated shipping costs, are considered for transfer to UNSOH, based on needs. A tranche of equipment, including armoured buses, as well as weapons and associated accessories for the UNSOH close protection team, will be transferred from UNAMI during the 2025/26 period.

27.	The freight forwarding, air transport and movement control concept assumes that commercial aviation and port infrastructure in Haiti will remain fragile, with no routine commercial flights to Port-au-Prince in the near term and gang-related disruptions to main supply routes and fuel terminals. UNSOH cargo will originate from multiple sources, including the United Nations Logistics Base, vendors and transfers from other missions. Most heavy cargo will be shipped by sea, and select urgent and sensitive cargo will be moved by chartered airlift.

28.	Significant start-up costs will be incurred during both periods, including mobilization costs for fuel and rations contracts; freight for contingent-owned equipment and for equipment from Brindisi; and the acquisition of safety and security equipment, vehicles, information and communications technology (ICT) equipment and medical equipment. The rations contract will be mobilized in three phases as the Support Office expands, with the first phase conducted during the 2025/26 period and the second and third phases during the 2026/27 period. The fuel contract includes mobilization costs and the establishment of a local strategic reserve. A strategic reserve for aviation fuel will be establishment during the 2025/26 period.
C.	Regional mission cooperation

29.	During the 2025/26 and the 2026/27 periods, UNSOH will engage with regional partners, including the Standing Group of Partners with regard to their role in providing high-level strategic direction, oversight and relevant political decision-making for the Gang Suppression Force, including on force generation and securing voluntary financial contributions; OAS with regard to the provision of technical support for its delivery of a targeted support package to the Haitian National Police and Haitian Armed Forces; and regional countries, including the Dominican Republic, with regard to the establishment of an UNSOH office for non-location-based transactional functions and medical support.

Note: The following abbreviations are used in the tables: ASG, Assistant Secretary-General; FS, Field Service; NGS, national General Service; NPO, National Professional Officer; USG, Under-Secretary-General; UNV, United Nations Volunteer.
D.	Human resources
Component: provision of logistical support
E.	Civilian staffing requirements
Staffing justifications – Office of the Head of the United Nations Support Office in Haiti

30.	The Head of UNSOH will be responsible for the implementation of the mandate of the Support Office. The Head of UNSOH will ensure the effective provision of operational support to the Gang Suppression Force, BINUH, the Haitian National Police and the Haitian Armed Forces on any joint operations with the Gang Suppression Force; and the implementation of the human rights due diligence policy. The Head of UNSOH will engage with Member States, the Standing Group of Partners, the Special Representative for the Force, the Force Commander, troop- and police-contributing countries and other United Nations agencies and partners on issues related to UNSOH mandate. The Head of UNSOH will also be responsible for managing strategic relationships with all United Nations entities, as well as local and international partners operating in Haiti.

31.	The immediate Office of the Head of UNSOH will provide support to the Head of UNSOH in all aspects of the incumbent’s daily work, including developing policy initiatives, identifying priority areas for action and tracking follow-up. The staff will also ensure coordination with, and the smooth flow of information to and from, the leadership of the Support Office, maintain relations with ministers, senior government officials, United Nations partners and the international community, manage correspondence, draft documents for signature and provide support for the day-to-day functioning of the immediate Office.

32.	Reporting directly to the Head of UNSOH will be the Conduct and Discipline Team, the Board of Inquiry, the Public Information Unit, the Legal Affairs Unit, the Human Rights Due Diligence Policy Unit and the Safety and Security Section. The Director of Mission Support will also report directly to the Head of UNSOH.

33.	The Conduct and Discipline Team will be the focal point on all conduct and discipline matters. It will provide policy guidance and technical advice to the Support Office on issues related to conduct and discipline. The Team will receive complaints and reports of misconduct and refer them to the appropriate entity. It will establish and maintain a database of all misconduct cases in the Support Office and conduct training for United Nations civilian personnel on conduct and discipline and on the prevention of sexual exploitation and abuse.
34.	The Board of Inquiry will be responsible for preparing Board reports, establishing rosters of cases and monitoring the implementation of all Board recommendations.

35.	The Public Information Unit will support the implementation of the mandate of the Support Office by acting as a centre for strategic communications between UNSOH and the population of Haiti, advancing the key objectives of the Office, ensuring a public understanding of the Office’s role, shaping its public image locally, regionally and internationally and supporting the dissemination of accurate information to the population. To that end, the Unit will develop and implement a strategic communications plan to undertake specific communications campaigns that reflect the overall strategy and priorities of the Office. Strategic communications will be critical from the outset, to establish UNSOH as a credible and impartial force on the ground.

36.	The Legal Affairs Unit will be the central and authoritative source of legal advice to the Support Office, including with regard to the Office’s mandate, the host country agreement and other agreements with neighbouring countries, the Convention on the Privileges and Immunities of the United Nations of 1946, the Convention on the Safety of United Nations and Associated Personnel of 1994 and relevant standards and rules of international law, including international humanitarian law, the laws and regulations of the host country and the overall legal and administrative framework of the Organization. The main tasks of the Legal Affairs Unit will include the interpretation of mandates, professional scrutiny of transactions, the drafting of legal opinions and other instruments, representation on boards and committees and representation of the Office before governmental bodies within and outside Haiti.

37.	The Human Rights Due Diligence Policy Unit will be responsible, in close cooperation with the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights and BINUH, for preventing the United Nations from providing support to non-United Nations security forces in contexts where there are credible risks of human rights violations. The key functions of the Unit will be: (a) providing policy advice to UNSOH leadership, coordinating UNSOH participation in the human rights due diligence policy task force, facilitating engagement with national authorities to secure cooperation with mitigation measures and liaising with the Head of the Human Rights Service of BINUH; (b) monitoring and reporting through field monitoring, verification of allegations and reporting on the conduct of recipients of United Nations support; and (c) risk assessment by developing human rights due diligence policy risk assessments, identifying and tracking mitigation measures, reassessing risks as operations evolve and supporting the Gang Suppression Force and national authorities in implementing mitigation measures.
Safety and Security Section

38.	The Safety and Security Section will be responsible for establishing and maintaining the security management system and managing the safety and security of UNSOH personnel, premises and assets, liaising with the Government and local authorities on all security matters. The main functions of the Section will be to conduct threat analysis and risk assessments, provide operational security support and emergency response on a 24/7 basis, provide security training and briefings to UNSOH personnel, direct the security guard force, conduct security investigations, provide personal protection services to senior officials of the Support Office and to high-level delegations and provide security clearances for travel into and within the mission area. The Section will also be responsible for monitoring compliance with the security risk management measures and the residential security measures approved by the designated official.
Office of the Director of Mission Support

39.	The Director of Mission Support will be responsible for the overall management and coordination of logistical, administrative and operational support functions necessary to enable the effective implementation of the mandate of UNSOH. The incumbent will serve as the principal adviser to the Head of UNSOH on all matters related to logistical, administrative, financial, contractual, technical and operational support and will support the implementation of UNSOH mandate, including through direct support provided to the Gang Suppression Force in key operational areas. The incumbent will be responsible for providing technical assistance to OAS and for overseeing casualty evacuations for the Haitian National Police and the Haitian Armed Forces during joint operations with the Gang Suppression Force, as well as for delivering the full range of support services to BINUH. The incumbent will ensure compliance with United Nations regulations, rules, policies and procedures. The incumbent will contribute to the integrated and consistent implementation of the mandate, while maintaining communication with Headquarters, the United Nations country team and coordination bodies, in order to address shared priorities and ensure operational alignment. The incumbent will be responsible for liaising, negotiating and coordinating with Haitian national authorities on administrative and supply chain matters and ensuring effective cooperation and adherence to host country agreements, while also collaboratively actively with the Force, the Haitian National Police, Haitian Armed Forces, BINUH and OAS in order to ensure coordinated and cohesive support aligned with operational requirements.

40.	The immediate Office of the Director of Mission Support will provide administrative, logistical and technical support to the Gang Suppression Force in the implementation of the mandate of the Support Office.

41.	Reporting directly to the Director of Mission Support are the Environmental Unit, the Aviation Safety Unit, the Audit Response Unit, the Claims Unit, the Welfare Unit, the Training Unit and the Gender Affairs Unit. The Chiefs of Operations and Resource Management, Service Delivery and Supply Chain Management also report directly to the Director of Mission Support.

42.	The Environmental Unit will develop and implement the UNSOH environmental policy, objectives and action plan. The Unit will conduct technical assessments of wastewater and solid waste systems; evaluate efficiency and compliance with environmental standards, with a view to an eventual handover to national authorities where possible and appropriate; establish efficient and effective treatment systems to ensure safe and sustainable operations; and monitor treated wastewater quality to ensure that it can be safely discharged in accordance with United Nations standards.

43.	The Aviation Safety Unit will establish and manage the UNSOH aviation safety programme to ensure that every effort is made to prevent accidents and incidents during aviation operations, by reducing associated risks to acceptable levels. Key responsibilities include conducting risk assessments for the main airfields and identifying and analysing potential threats to aviation activities; making safety recommendations to the respective stakeholders; performing initial aircraft acceptance inspections for all newly arrived aircraft; providing performance aviation safety briefings to all incoming crews; and implementing an effective hazard and occurrence reporting system, investigating all significant hazards and incidents and recommending appropriate mitigation measures.

44.	The Audit Response Unit will act as the focal point for audit-related issues involving the Support Office. The Unit will support the work of the oversight bodies; facilitate briefings to the oversight bodies on UNSOH policies, procedures, guidelines and systems; coordinate and prepare responses to oversight queries and reports; monitor the implementation of oversight recommendations and guide offices on the actions required to implement outstanding oversight recommendations; and prepare quarterly reports on the status of implementation of outstanding oversight recommendations.

45.	The Claims Unit will support the property survey board, the local contracts committee and claims review boards, prepare reports for the boards, establish rosters of cases and monitor the implementation of all Board of Inquiry recommendations.

46.	The Welfare Unit will be responsible for developing and implementing the welfare strategy of the Support Office and for providing counselling support to UNSOH personnel in consultation with the Medical Services Section. Its key function will be to ensure a healthy working, living and recreational environment for all Office personnel.

47.	The Training Unit will be responsible for identifying training requirements and for developing, implementing and monitoring training programmes to build organizational and individual capacities to assist with the achievement of mandated responsibilities.

48.	The Gender Affairs Unit will provide strategic advice and technical support to UNSOH on developing effective strategies to mainstream gender equality and the women, peace and security mandate throughout the Support Office; strengthen the capacity of personnel to advance gender equality and the women and peace and security mandates; and assist senior leadership in monitoring progress and ensuring accountability and compliance by all personnel.
Office of the Chief of Operations and Resource Management

49.	The Chief of Operations and Resource Management will support the Director of Mission Support in the management of financial and human resources and the provision of ICT services. Reporting to the Chief of Operations and Resource Management are the Finance and Budget Section, the Human Resources Section and the Field Technology Section.
Finance and Budget Section

50.	The Finance and Budget Section will provide strategic advice to the senior leadership on budgetary and financial matters; provide guidance for budget preparation; maintain the accounts of the Support Office; monitor and approve obligations and disbursements; ensure that funds are effectively used for the purpose for which they were approved; implement financial transactions and controls; disburse funds in settlement of invoices; administer the Office’s bank accounts and petty cash; and ensure compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations and the International Public Sector Accounting Standards.
Human Resources Section

51.	The Human Resources Section will be responsible for the provision of integrated and strategic human resources management services to the Support Office, including planning for staff requirements, recruitment, placement, administration, training services and capacity-building and providing strategic advice to the senior leadership and hiring managers on human resources planning, management, policy implementation and monitoring. The Section will prepare and review job descriptions, establish and administer the work of local permanent and ad hoc panels and administer all official travel and visa requirements for civilian staff.
Field Technology Section

52.	The Field Technology Section will have overall responsibility and oversight for the provision of geospatial, information and communications technology services to all personnel in the mission area, including ensuring secure, resilient and mission-critical communications in support of the Gang Suppression Force and UNSOH. The Section will oversee the design, implementation and integration of ICT systems that meet the operational requirements of the Support Office and the Force and deliver end-to-end support for data, voice, video and web services, enterprise network management, information assurance and strategic communications.
Office of the Chief of Service Delivery

53.	The Chief of Service Delivery will plan, coordinate and track complex projects with the aim of ensuring effective implementation of the mandate of the Support Office. Reporting to the Chief of Service Delivery are the Medical Services Section, the Life Support Section, the Engineering and Facilities Management Section, the Aviation Section, the Transport Section and the Mission Support Centre.
Medical Services Section

54.	The Medical Services Section will provide medical services to all UNSOH, BINUH and Gang Suppression Force personnel, including health maintenance and preventive medical treatment services; coordinate medical and casualty evacuations within and outside the mission area; and plan for medical contingencies.
Life Support Section

55.	The Life Support Section will manage the fuel, rations and general supply operations of the Support Office. The Section will plan and manage the uninterrupted supply of fuel and rations, as well as equipment and consumables; oversee contractor performance and ensure compliance with quality, health and environmental standards; and plan and ensure minimum operating stocks.
Engineering and Facilities Management Section

56.	The Engineering and Facilities Management Section will be responsible for providing overall administrative and technical direction for engineering operations throughout the Support Office. The Section will ensure that the Office’s office premises, logistics bases, accommodation facilities and all other established infrastructure and facilities are maintained and will establish facilities and infrastructure at all UNSOH locations, in full compliance with minimum operating security standards. Engineering services will include the construction and renovation of facilities and the provision of bulk water, electricity, sanitation, water treatment, wastewater treatment and facilities management.
Aviation Section

57.	The Aviation Section will be responsible for the management and efficient and safe utilization of the aviation assets of the Support Office in support of the UNSOH mandate. The Section will plan and coordinate the utilization of air assets, ensure compliance with regulations and standard operating procedures, manage air assets contracts, manage air terminal operations, manage airfields and liaise with national and international aviation authorities.
Transport Section

58.	The Transport Section will be responsible for the management and maintenance of the surface transport assets of the Support Office. The Section will plan, organize and control vehicular transportation services, the maintenance and repair of the vehicle fleet and the operation of the vehicle workshops; allocate and distribute vehicles; formulate and implement road safety standards and procedures; and provide passenger transport and dispatch services.
Mission Support Centre

59.	The Mission Support Centre will coordinate and prioritize all logistics requirements, including the tasking of relevant service delivery sections and military enablers, in cooperation with the Gang Suppression Force. The Centre will provide operational planning guidance in order to ensure that tasks and objectives are well defined and fully resourced. While the primary role of the Centre is to ensure the provision of efficient, timely and effective support to UNSOH and the Force, it will also deal with aspects of cooperation and mutual assistance between the Support Office and other United Nations agencies and non-governmental organizations. The Centre will also coordinate and monitor the deployment, rotation and repatriation of UNSOH elements. The Centre will oversee the Contingent-Owned Equipment Unit, which will be responsible for the inspections of contingent-owned equipment and self-sustainment capabilities, the maintenance of the contingent-owned equipment and ammunition databases and the provision of guidance to contingent personnel on United Nations contingent-owned equipment policies and procedures.
Office of the Chief of Supply Chain Management

60.	The Chief of Supply Chain Management will direct and supervise the work of the Supply Chain Acquisition and Performance Management Section, the Procurement Section, the Movement Control Section and the Centralized Warehouse Section.
Supply Chain Acquisition and Performance Management Section

61.	The Supply Chain Acquisition and Performance Management Section will serve as the supply chain planning focal point for the Support Office and will enhance the accountability, transparency and operational efficiency of the supply chain by strengthening compliance, optimizing operational performance and ensuring the timely delivery of mission-critical services. The Section will manage the acquisition process by centrally coordinating acquisition planning and requisitioning and by developing acquisition strategies for services and commodities, considering sourcing options, market conditions, logistics requirements, demand forecast, inventory levels and operational priorities. The Section will analyse end-to-end supply chain performance, identify potential challenges and opportunities, implement the supply chain performance management framework and provide data-driven reports to support informed decision-making. The Section will ensure strict adherence to the Financial Regulations and Rules, International Public Sector Accounting Standards and the delegation of authority for property-related activities, including accurate reporting of property, plant and equipment and financial inventory.
Procurement Section

62.	The Procurement Section will provide cost-effective, efficient, timely and accurate procurement support to UNSOH in accordance with the Financial Regulations and Rules and the Procurement Manual. This will include the optimization of the acquisition process through effective procurement planning, service delivery and communication to clients, as well as through the provision of expert procurement advice to senior management on matters of regional and international procurement and supply chain management initiatives.
Movement Control Section

63.	The Movement Control Section will be responsible for the planning and execution of all movements of personnel and cargo within the mission area; the deployment, rotation and repatriation of military contingents and formed police units, contingent-owned and United Nations-owned equipment; customs clearing and freight forwarding of personal effects; and passenger and cargo handling and transit warehousing operations at the airport and the logistics base.
Centralized Warehouse Section

64.	The Centralized Warehouse Section will provide the Support Office with a cost-effective and efficient supply of assets and commodities. The Section will cover a wide range of activities, including receiving and inspection, warehousing and distribution of assets, inspection of returned assets and the initiation of the write-off and disposal of obsolete assets in compliance with the approved disposal method. The Section will manage UNSOH assets and inventory, including forecasting, planning, replenishing and monitoring inventory, and will physically manage all assets and consumables, including the receipt, storage, control, picking, packing, staging and distribution thereof in a timely manner.

Component: provision of logistical support

table_content.text: 1.1	Rapid, effective, efficient and responsible delivery of the United Nations logistics support package to the Gang Suppression Force

Several factors may have an impact on the ability to deliver proposed outputs as planned, including deterioration of the political, security, economic and humanitarian situations; adverse weather conditions; other instances of force majeure; variance in host government compliance with the provisions of the status-of-forces/mission agreement; gaps in capabilities generated by troop- or police-contributing countries with regard to the proposed forces configuration; inability to obtain all necessary clearances for the employment of selected civilian staff candidates; and inability of vendors, contractors and suppliers to deliver goods and services

children.text: A.	Overall
(Thousands of United States dollars; budget year is from 1 July to 30 June)
B.	Non-budgeted contributions
65.	The value of non-budgeted contributions for the periods from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 and from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 will be presented once the status-of-mission agreement is signed.
C.	Vacancy factors
66.	The cost estimates for the periods from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026 and from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 take into account the vacancy factors outlined in the table below.
(Percentage)
a	Based on a 12-month adjusted vacancy rate.

67.	The vacancy rate for the 2025/26 period is based on a 50 per cent vacancy rate and the deployment of staff for 6 months, resulting in a 12-month adjusted vacancy rate of 75 per cent for civilian personnel. The vacancy rate for the 2026/27 period is based on the estimated deployment of staff in view of the current pace of recruitment.
D.	Analysis of requirements for air operations

68.	The flight hour utilization rates for the periods from 2022/23 to 2024/25, the approved and proposed number of flight hours for the 2025/26 and 2026/27 periods, respectively, and the number of flight hours, calculated on the basis of the policy guidance, are as follows:
69.	The Mission will deploy 12 aircraft during the 2025/26 budget period and 13 aircraft during the 2026/27 budget period.

70.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover: (a) the travel on emplacement of 3,360 military contingent personnel (1,500 by 1 April 2026 and 1,860 by 1 July 2026); (b) the provision of rations for 1,500 military contingent personnel for three months, reserves of bottled water and rations for 14 days and mobilization and transportation fees; (c) the reimbursement of contingent-owned equipment for 1,500 troops for three months; and (d) the cost of freight for the deployment of contingent-owned equipment.

71.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) an increase in the number of troops from 1,500 in the 2025/26 period to 3,360 as of 1 July 2026 and 4,940 as of 1 January 2027; (b) the provision of rations for 12 months in the 2026/27 period compared with 3 months in the 2025/26 period; (c) the reimbursement of contingent-owned equipment for 12 months in the 2026/27 period compared with 3 months in the 2025/26 period; and (d) the first rotation of 3,360 troops deployed in the 2025/26 period.
72.	The increased requirements are offset in part by lower freight costs due to a reduction in the amount of contingent-owned equipment deployed.

73.	The requirements cover: (a) the travel on emplacement of 140 formed police unit personnel; (b) the provision of rations for 140 formed police unit personnel for three months, as well as reserves of bottled water and rations for 14 days; (c) the reimbursement of contingent-owned equipment for 140 police officers in formed units for three months; and (d) the cost of freight for the deployment of contingent-owned equipment.

74.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) an increase in the number of formed police unit personnel from 140 in the 2025/26 period to 560 as of 1 January 2027; (b) the provision of rations for 12 months in the 2026/27 period compared with 3 months in the 2025/26 period; (c) the reimbursement of contingent-owned equipment for 12 months in the 2026/27 period compared with 3 months in the 2025/26 period; and (d) the deployment of an additional 420 formed police unit personnel and their equipment.

75.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover the salaries, staff assessment and common staff costs of 264 international staff, namely 1 Assistant Secretary-General, 1 D-2, 3 D-1, 16 P-5, 40 P-4, 57 P-3, 7 P-2 and 139 Field Service, with the application of a vacancy rate of 75 per cent. The requirements also cover the danger pay entitlement of 236 international staff based in Port-au-Prince. The requirements take into account an amount of $1,258,600 to be recovered from BINUH for mission support services provided by UNSOH.

76.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the application of a lower vacancy rate of 30 per cent compared with a vacancy rate of 75 per cent proposed for the 2025/26 period.

77.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover the salaries, staff assessment and common staff costs of 23 National Professional Officers, with the application of a vacancy rate of 75 per cent. The requirements also cover the danger pay entitlement of 19 National Professional Officers based in Port-au-Prince. The requirements take into account an amount of $22,900 to be recovered from BINUH for mission support services provided by UNSOH.

78.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the application of a lower vacancy rate of 30 per cent compared with a vacancy rate of 75 per cent proposed for the 2025/26 period.

79.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover the salaries, staff assessment and common staff costs of 132 national General Service staff, with the application of a vacancy rate of 75 per cent. The requirements also cover the danger pay entitlement of 117 national General Service staff based in Port-au-Prince. The requirements take into account an amount of $150,500 to be recovered from BINUH for mission support services provided by UNSOH.

80.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the application of a lower vacancy rate of 30 per cent compared with a vacancy rate of 75 per cent proposed for the 2025/26 period.
81.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover the cost of a videographer for a video project to document the establishment of UNSOH and the deployment of the Gang Suppression Force.
82.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the recruitment of trainers for a training course on organizational governance.

83.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover the travel of staff on temporary duty assignment during the start-up of the Support Office; security and road assessment missions in the field; the travel of senior officials and their close protection officers in the field; and travel for security training.

84.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to discontinuation of the provision for travel of staff on temporary duty assignment during the start-up of the Support Office.

85.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover: (a) the first-year construction costs of a multi-year infrastructure project to support 5,500 uniformed and 50 civilian personnel of the Gang Suppression Force and UNSOH civilian personnel, providing accommodation, office space, medical facilities, warehouses and camps, with activities such as ground clearance, perimeter security, hardwall structures, and the fit-out and installation of treatment plants; and (b) the acquisition of prefabricated industrial and residential buildings and structures, cooling accessories, exterior lighting fixtures, cooking and warming equipment, refrigerators, generators and electrical equipment, water treatment equipment, tanks and cylinders, pumps, electrical wires and cables, and various consumables in support of the multi-year infrastructure project.

86.	The requirements also cover: (a) material and field defence supplies for the security of camps, and field defence stores for troops of the Gang Suppression Force for checkpoints, mobile patrol duties and forward operating bases; (b) the maintenance costs and spare parts for wastewater treatment plants, waste management equipment, generators, air conditioners, bulk water treatment plants and various other pieces of equipment; (c) cleaning services, building maintenance and gardening, hydrology investigation services, fumigation and pest control, and laundry services; (d) the acquisition of personal safety and protection equipment, security surveillance and detection equipment, arms and ammunition, various safety and security supplies, and the provision of security guards for UNSOH headquarters in Port-au-Prince and the office in Santo Domingo; (e) the rental of premises in Port-au-Prince and office space in Santo Domingo; (f) the cost of water, electricity and cleaning services for offices in Port-au-Prince and Santo Domingo, and disposal services for solid, liquid and hazardous material; and (g) the provision of fuel for generators for UNSOH and the Gang Suppression Force.

87.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the continued implementation of the multi-year infrastructure project to support 5,500 uniformed and 50 civilian personnel of the Gang Suppression Force and UNSOH civilian personnel, with expansion in the regions and the implementation of a renewable energy project that includes the installation of turnkey solar hybrid systems; and (b) the provision of utilities and waste disposal services, maintenance services, security services, spare parts and supplies, the rental of premises, and fuel for 12 months in the 2026/27 period compared with 6 months in the 2025/26 period.
88.	The increased requirements are offset in part by discontinuation of the one-time provision for the acquisition of equipment for the start-up of the Support Office.

89.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover the acquisition of 30 light passenger vehicles, 1 bus, 5 vans and 62 armoured vehicles to support the mobility of UNSOH and civilian personnel of the Gang Suppression Force, as well as 24 trucks for various uses, 4 ambulances, including 3 armoured ones, 3 armoured personnel carriers, 9 pieces of engineering equipment, 23 pieces of material handling equipment, and 29 trailers and vehicle attachments; the rental of material-handling equipment used for construction projects and of buses and light vehicles in Santo Domingo for the transportation of personnel; the acquisition of workshop tools, as well as CarLog kits and software; the provision of fuel for vehicles for UNSOH and the Gang Suppression Force; and spare parts, repairs and maintenance and insurance of the vehicle fleet.
90.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to discontinuation of the one-time provision for the acquisition of vehicles for the start-up of the Support Office.

91.	The reduced requirements are offset in part by increased requirements for repairs and maintenance, spare parts and fuel as a result of an increase in the vehicle fleet and the Support Office operating for 12 months in the 2026/27 period.

92.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover the cost of the deployment of one fixed-wing aircraft positioned in Santo Domingo as of January 2026, two civilian medium utility helicopters as of January and March 2026 and eight military utility helicopters by the end of June 2026; the cost of 1,047 flight hours and the related fuel; monthly operation and maintenance costs, and one-time transportation and delivery costs in relation to the fuel contract; insurance for the fleet; daily subsistence allowance for crew members; airport charges; the cost of the aviation information management system for one fixed-wing aircraft and two helicopters; and various aviation equipment and supplies.

93.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) an increase in the aviation fleet by one fixed-wing aircraft; and (b) an increase in the number of flight hours from 1,047 in the 2025/26 period to 5,190 in the 2026/27 period as a result of the Support Office operating for 12 months in the 2026/27 period.
94.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover the cost of sea containers for the transportation of equipment acquired or transferred from strategic deployment stocks and closing missions.
95.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the lower number of sea containers needed as a result of the reduction in acquisitions.

96.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover the acquisition of network and storage equipment, videoconference equipment, uninterruptible power supply, servers, notebook and desktop computers, computer displays and computer accessories; the installation of communications equipment, including modular telecommunication modules, base telecommunication radio stations, closed-circuit television, public address systems, counter systems to unmanned aerial systems, and local area network and fibre optic cabling; communication services, including wide area network, internet services, satellite transponder services, radio frequency track system, Starlink data package, and fixed and mobile phone services; and information security software, geographical information system (GIS) licences and geodata acquisition and specialized software, and telemedicine capability.

97.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to discontinuation of the one-time provision for the acquisition and installation of equipment for the start-up of the Support Office.

98.	The reduced requirements are offset in part by increased requirements for communication services and the rental of an increased number of telecommunications towers and microwave hubs as a result of the increase in the number of troops and the number of locations.

99.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover the establishment of a turnkey level II-plus medical facility in Port-au-Prince through commercial arrangements; specialized services, including X-ray and laboratory; medical evacuation through air ambulance; commercial arrangements with treatment facilities in the Dominican Republic and other regional locations; the acquisition of cholera vaccines for Gang Suppression Force and UNSOH personnel, along with condoms, blood supplies and other medicines and consumables; and the acquisition of equipment for the United Nations level I clinic.

100.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the provision of various medical services, including services provided by the turnkey level II-plus medical facility for 12 months in the 2026/27 period compared with 3 to 6 months in the 2025/26 period.
101.	The increased requirements are offset in part by discontinuation of the one-time provision for the acquisition of equipment for the start-up of the Support Office.

102.	For the 2025/26 period, the requirements cover the cost of sea and air freight for equipment and supplies from vendors or transferred from strategic deployment stocks and closing missions; training fees for mandatory security training; the acquisition of packing materials and gym equipment; individual contractors; and the cost of general insurance, external audits, operational maps and bank charges.

103.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) a reduction in freight costs as a result of a reduction in acquisitions; and (b) discontinuation of the one-time provision for the acquisition of packing materials for the start-up of the Support Office.

104.	The reduced requirements are offset in part by increased requirements due to: (a) the provision of general insurance for 12 months in the 2026/27 period compared with 6 months in the 2025/26 period; and (b) the provision of individual contractors for 12 months in the 2026/27 period compared with 3 months in the 2025/26 period.

text: IV.	Actions to be taken by the General Assembly

children.text: 105.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly in connection with the financing of the United Nations Support Office in Haiti are:
(a)	To appropriate the amount of $271,328,700 for the maintenance of the Support Office for the 9-month period from 1 October 2025 to 30 June 2026;
(b)	To appropriate the amount of $403,583,200 for the maintenance of the Support Office for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027;
(c)	To assess the amount in subparagraph (b) above at a monthly rate of $33,631,933 should the Security Council decide to continue the mandate of the Support Office.
Annex – Organization chart
Abbreviations: ASG, Assistant Secretary-General; FS, Field Service; NGS, national General Service; NPO, National Professional Officer.
